topic: The state of open-weight LLMs in 2026: who leads on benchmarks, licensing, and adoption?
probes: The state of open-weight LLMs in 2026: who leads on benchmarks, licensing, and adoption?
Which open-weight LLMs achieved the highest average scores across standardized academic benchmarks (e.g., MMLU, GSM8K, HumanEval, ARC, TruthfulQA) in peer-reviewed or widely cited 2026 evaluations?

What are the exact license types (e.g., Apache 2.0, MIT, Llama 3 Community License, custom terms) applied to the top five most-downloaded open-weight LLMs released or updated in 2026, and do any impose usage restrictions beyond standard open-source definitions?

What percentage of production AI applications deployed by Fortune 500 companies in 2026 use at least one open-weight LLM, and which specific models are most frequently named in public technical documentation or case studies?

Which organization or consortium released the highest-performing open-weight LLM on the 2026 Hugging Face Open LLM Leaderboard (or equivalent authoritative benchmark aggregation), and what is its architecture, parameter count, and training data cutoff date?

How many distinct open-weight LLMs released in 2026 have been integrated into at least three major open-source inference or orchestration frameworks (e.g., vLLM, Ollama, llama.cpp, Text Generation Inference) as verified by merged pull requests or official model support lists?

What is the median time from initial public release to first stable version (v1.0 or equivalent) for open-weight LLMs launched in 2026, calculated from GitHub release dates or official model card publication dates?

society.report: In 2026, the landscape of open-weight large language models (LLMs) is characterized by a diverse array of models with varying architectures, licensing, and adoption. Notable models include Qwen3-235B-A22B from Alibaba, which boasts 235B total parameters and 22B active per token, and is licensed under Apache 2.0 (finding_id: 05d34304f42941cebecc354650192113). OpenAI has also released gpt-oss-20b and gpt-oss-120b, both under Apache 2.0, with memory requirements of 16 GB and 80 GB, respectively (finding_id: 5d36159cfa6245b1942f68b328ec6849). Google's Gemma 4 12B Unified, another Apache 2.0 model, features an encoder-free multimodal architecture and has achieved strong performance on benchmarks such as AIME_2026 (77.5) and MMLU_Pro (77.2) (finding_id: 0a0fef2b9fc04c3c978c34dd0156e5a1). Mistral Medium 3.5 128B, released under a Modified MIT license with a revenue cap, unifies reasoning, instruct, and coding capabilities (finding_id: 8332757e3c694e44bf07932645ed4b42). WebWorld-8B/14B/32B, developed by the Qwen Team, claims to outperform GPT-4o in factuality (finding_id: 81ecc34ca38d4ed287c5f8f7de7201cf). Covenant-72B, a pre-trained model via a permissionless P2P network, has shown competitive benchmark performance, including 56.8 on ARC_Challenge (finding_id: 0dfeb7f527df41a49bc126777ebfafe4). JetBrains' Mellum 2 12B-A2.5B, a MoE code model, has been trained using RLVR and is designed for IDE-resident inference (finding_id: 4f48f097215048958e1b59ad31ce5402). Sapient Intelligence's HRM-Text-1B, a Hierarchical Reasoning Model, is the first of its kind with a dual-timescale recurrent architecture (finding_id: 76211d80f2434842a7aa598a91850088). In terms of benchmarks, Medmarks-V, a verifiable subset of Medmarks, has seen Gemini 3 Pro Preview as the top performer with a win rate of 0.6628 (finding_id: 41fec2186b984700aafa2056e9c0c40d). The financial services industry, in particular, has seen significant AI adoption, with 65% of firms actively using AI, and 84% considering open-source models central to their strategy (finding_id: 6c8473b5feea494a94d2a2bdd875b00a, finding_id: c1fd2b50d9ff427f9a1602a9fe0477e4). Despite being 90% cheaper, open-source models account for less than 30% of the market share (finding_id: 0c10b4dff2914ec3a6d5cfc8c667ffb0).
